coredata.dc:title: Emergence of robust carbon quantum dots as nano-tracer for groundwater studies☆
coredata.dc:creator: Warsi, Taufique
Bhattacharjee, Lopamudra
Thangamani, S.
Jat, Sanjeev K.
Mohanta, Kallol
Bhattacharjee, Rama Ranjan
Ramaswamy, Rangarajan
Manikyamba, C.
Rao, T. Vinoda
coredata.dc:description: Chemical tracers, both artificial and natural, are the established tools for providing information on groundwater flow characteristics such as groundwater flow velocity and flow direction. Chemicals namely chloride, bromide, iodide, dye tracers (Rhodamine-WT) and radioactive elements (Tritium) are artificial tracers that have been used as injected tracers for hydrogeological studies. However, the use of conventional tracers are often limited due to high diffusion rates and difficulties in estimating very low water flow velocities of the order 0.00001 to 0.001 m/s. Herein, we report the first ever large scale synthesis and testing of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as hydrogeological tracers. CQDs, as they are now popularly termed, are a new class of environmentally friendly and highly fluorescent nanomaterial mainly used for bio-imaging and catalytic applications. In this manuscript, we have used fluorescence emission and surface functionalization of the CQDs as parameters to study flow of water in simulated laboratory columns and in field. The robustness of the CQDs towards environmental factors has been tested. Comparative tracer results suggested that CQDs may be envisaged as nano-tracers for future hydrogeological investigations.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (PSS-CQDs) in large-scale for laboratory & field test
Preparation of CQD-tracer standards and calibration
Column preparation for lab scale tracer studies
Preparation of CQD standards and calibration using aquifer water
Bore hole tracer (aquifer field tracer) tests
Borehole test for groundwater velocity estimation using CQD tracer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TGA of PSS-CQDs prepared at in tubular furnace (small-scale) and in muffle furnace (large-scale); (b) FTIR spectra of PSS-CQDs prepared in tubular furnace (small-scale) and in muffle furnace at (large-scale); (c) Fluorescence excitation & emission spectra of the PSS-CQDs & pictorial representation of the blue emitting CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
2D AFM image of the spin-coated CQDs.
pH dependency of emission spectra of PSS-CQDs. Spectra recorded after excitation at 340 nm. (a) Emission sopectra at different pH; (b) Fluorescence maxima obtained from (a) is plotted w.r.t. pH.
(a) Emission spectra of PSS-CQDs dispersed in alkali & acid; (b) Emission spectra of PSS-CQDs recorded after temperature & pressure treatment. All spectra were recorded at excitation of 340 nm.
Concentration dependant emission spectra of PSS-CQD dispersion in water. All the spectra are obtained at excitation of 340 nm.

(a) EPI-fluorescence image of drop casted PSS-CQDs under UV-filter; (b, c & d) EPI-fluorescence image of spin-coated PSS-CQDs under UV, blue and green filter respectively. All images were obtained at 20× magnification. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Effect of Cu2+ addition on emission property of PSS-CQDs (5 ppm). Excitation wavelength = 340 nm. (b) Effect of Cu2+ addition on emission property of humic acid (10 ppm). Excitation wavelength = 340 nm.

a) Breakthrough curve of three combined tracers (CP, tritium, chloride) in column A, b) Breakthrough curve of two combined tracers (CP + Tritium) for column B, c) Breakthrough curves of comparative tracers' movement for coarse textured red soil (Column C), d) Breakthrough curves of comparative tracers' movement for medium textured red soil (Column D). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cluster bore wells site at Maheshwaram, Rangareddy district, Telangana, India.
(a) PSS-CQD concentration of the observation well BW2 for exp. 1 (b) PSS-CQD concentration of the observation well BW1 for exp. 2.
Characteristics of fabricated columns for lab experiments.

row.entry: 100 ml of 0.1 g of carbon sample = 1000 ppm + 10 ml of tritium-25 μCi/ml = 125 microcurie
100 ml of 0.1 g of carbon sample = 1000 ppm + 100 ml of 2000 ppm chloride +10 ml of tritium-25 μCi/ml = 125 microcurie
(Column C) Coarse textured red soil coarse sand: 65% (2000–500 μm), medium sand: 32% (500–250 μm), fine sand: 2.5% (250–60 μm), silt and clay: 0.5% (<60 μm)
100 mg of CQD tracer in 100 ml water = 1000 ppm CQD tracer +2 ml of 25 microcurie/ml tritium = 50 microcurie
(Column D) Medium textured red soil coarse sand: 38% (2000–500 μm), medium sand: 56% (500–250 μm), fine sand: 4% (250–60 μm), silt and clay: 2% (<60 μm)
100 mg of CQD tracer in 200 ml water = 500 ppm + 5 ml of 25 micro curie/ml tritium = 125 microcurie

head.ce:title: Emergence of robust carbon quantum dots as nano-tracer for groundwater studies☆

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Geophysical Research Institute, Hyderabad, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Kolkata, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Kolkata

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Rama Ranjan Bhattacharjee, PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical tracers, both artificial and natural, are the established tools for providing information on groundwater flow characteristics such as groundwater flow velocity and flow direction. Chemicals namely chloride, bromide, iodide, dye tracers (Rhodamine-WT) and radioactive elements (Tritium) are artificial tracers that have been used as injected tracers for hydrogeological studies. However, the use of conventional tracers are often limited due to high diffusion rates and difficulties in estimating very low water flow velocities of the order 0.00001 to 0.001 m/s. Herein, we report the first ever large scale synthesis and testing of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as hydrogeological tracers. CQDs, as they are now popularly termed, are a new class of environmentally friendly and highly fluorescent nanomaterial mainly used for bio-imaging and catalytic applications. In this manuscript, we have used fluorescence emission and surface functionalization of the CQDs as parameters to study flow of water in simulated laboratory columns and in field. The robustness of the CQDs towards environmental factors has been tested. Comparative tracer results suggested that CQDs may be envisaged as nano-tracers for future hydrogeological investigations.

ce:section.ce:para: The demand for creative ways to predict availability and sustainability of our underground and surface water resources is necessary. Isotope and tracer techniques provide critical parameter on source, age, interconnectivity, flow rate and direction of groundwater along with its migration rate [1–8]. The same techniques can also provide information on the pathways of contaminants present in ground water on a local and regional scale levels for better water quality management and remedial measures [9,10]. The arrival concentration and arrival time of tracers can be used in determining ground water movement as well as diffusion of contaminants [4,11–16]. Tracer technology is the only technology which can provide the information regarding the contaminant “plume dimension”, direction and its vector [3,8–10].

The most commonly used chemical tracers that are added to ground water are radioactive substances, salts and fluorescent dyes. There are certain factors that may limit the use of conventional radioactive elements and molecular tracers [13,14,17–20]. The use of radioactive elements as tracers has potential hazardous effects on the environment especially on human beings and other animals, for which it is currently replaced by non-radioactive elements. The issue with non- radioactive molecular tracers is their dimension. The diffusion coefficient values of these tracers are very high for their molecular dimensions as per Newton's law of diffusion [19,21,22]. Hence, when these tracers are injected in to bore-wells, they diffuse into the rock strata and require long time to eventually come out of the bore-wells and some of the tracers are highly interactive and alter in the subsurface which cannot be considered as a tracer [23–26]. Thus, analysis of ground water flow and contaminant detection becomes time consuming and more expensive. Molecular contaminants are also used as tracers, but due to their size, they penetrate and diffuse very fast. Thus retrieving tracer samples and their analysis becomes troublesome.

Colloidal water-dispersible nanoparticles have unique physical and chemical properties. They are heavily used in applications such as drug delivery and bio labeling [27–32]. The properties that complement such applications include easy detection, controlled diffusion, chemical inertness and non-toxic nature. Colloidal dimensions of nanoparticles provide lower values of diffusion coefficient than molecular tracers and hence their extent of diffusion is far less than the molecular tracers [29]. The nanoparticles may not diffuse from the channels of fluid flow into the adjacent rock as do chemical tracers. The nano-tracers can therefore reveal how a seep fluid leaving one part of a well short-circuits to a recovery well after only perhaps a week rather than months of injection [28,29]. Furthermore, comparing the arrival of chemical and nanoparticle tracers, one can show the extent of channeled flow which can be important for certain contaminant remediation. Since nanoparticles can be tagged with bar codes of different fluorescent colour, a many tracer experiments could be run simultaneously in a reservoir, providing a great deal of information about fluid movements [33]. The data can be useful to track contaminant plume movement though reports on such studies with colloidal nanoparticles are rare [34]. Further, nanoparticles have unique detection abilities by spectrophotometry due to electron confinement effects and hence can be easily used for detection. Our group recently developed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which are new and interesting class of nanomaterials and possess interesting optical and electronic properties [35–37]. In the present manuscript, we have explored the feasibility of polystyrene sulphonate-coated CQDs (PSS-CQDs) to be used as hydrogeological tracer.

Citric acid (CA) (99.7%) was purchased from Himedia Chemicals. Poly (sodium 4-styrene sulfonate, 99.8%, (M.W. 70, 000 & 10,00,000) (PSS) and 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (1,8-ANS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and they were used as received without further purification. All acids and bases used were purchased from Loba and used as received. Dialysis membranes were purchased from Himedia. Milli-Q water was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (PSS-CQDs) in large-scale for laboratory & field test
ce:section.ce:para: 90 g of citric acid is dissolved in water in a beaker. In another beaker 15 g PSS polymer was dissolved in water. Both the solutions were stirred vigorously for few hs and mixed together. After mixing, solution was kept on stirring for 2 h and kept on water bath for drying at 75 °C. Evaporation of water produces a honey like liquid mixture of citric acid and PSS. The mixture was kept in vacuum oven at 60 °C for one day. After drying, the mass was pyrolyzed in muffle furnace at 200 °C for 2 h. Produced mass was taken out of furnace as it comes down to room temperature after pyrolysis. Product was black colour powder (PSS-CQDs) having approximate weight of 18 g. In the same way 5 batches of samples were prepared for tracer tests to scale up the amount of CQDs. This was required for laboratory and field tests. Gravimetric and thermogravimetric estimations indicate that 53% (w/v) of the PSS-CQDs are PSS and the rest (47% w/v) are pure CQDs. PSS do not contribute to any fluorescence emission but its role is to passivate the surface of the CQDs in order to control its nano-domain as well as increase the dispersibility of the CQDs in water.

0.1 g of PSS-CQDs mixed in 100 ml deionized water and was stirred continuously using magnetic stirrer for 2 days. This was used for preparation of 1000 mg/l and 500 mg/l PSS-CQD tracer. Tritium activity of 5 mCi/ml was used for preparation of 25 microcurie/ml tritium tracer. Analar grade sodium chloride powder was used for preparation of 2000 mg/l chloride tracer. The combination of these tracers was used to study their relative movement in the geological medium under simulated conditions. Before the column experiments, standards of various concentrations were prepared for calibrating the equipment fluorescence spectrometer and to know the order of fluorescence emission intensity values for various concentrations (Supplementary Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD-tracer standards and calibration
ce:section.ce:para: The PSS-CQD material obtained through synthesis was used for preparation of various standards and for its testing and calibration. 0.2 g of vacuum packed PSS-CQD sample is mixed with 200 ml of double distilled water in 500 ml beaker to make 1000 ppm concentration. The content is stirred in magnetic stirrer for 6 h. Various concentrations from 100 ppm to 5 ppb were prepared from the main standard through progressive dilution (Supplementary Table 1a–d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Column preparation for lab scale tracer studies
ce:section.ce:para: Column A was packed with fine sand, column B with coarse sand, column C with coarse textured red soil and column D with medium textured red soil (Table 1 ). The height and diameter of the PVC columns are 1.86 m and 0.2 m respectively. Closed PVC cap with holes and wire mesh were provided in the bottom of the tube for the infiltrated water to escape from the column. PVC mesh was also provided at the top of the sand column. About 10 cm open space above the top surface of soil/medium is provided for storing input water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD standards and calibration using aquifer water
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of vacuum packed PSS-CQD sample is mixed with 200 ml of aquifer water of the experimental site (Maheshwaram watershed, Telengana, India) in 500 ml beaker to make 1000 ppm concentration. The aquifer water is obtained after the bore well is pumped for >1 h. The content is stirred in magnetic stirrer for 6 h. Various concentrations from 1 to 10 ppm were prepared from this main standard through progressive dilution using aquifer water in the following table and figure (Supplementary Table 2 & Supplementary Fig. S2 & S3).

The samples were analysed in LS55-Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer with the instrument setting as; Excitation: 340 nm, Emission range: 360–550 nm, Excitation slit width: 3.5 nm, Emission slit width: 3.5 nm, scan speed: 100 nm/min. The standards were measured with above parameters. The emission intensity data obtained for various standards were correlated. The best fit line drawn and the regression equations shows linear correlation with the correlation coefficient of 0.99.

The samples were analysed in LS55-Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer which provides emission intensity and thus concentration of PSS-CQD. From the various trial experiments carried out, the excitation energy, emission energy range, excitation slit width, emission slit width, scan speed was identified for determining the actual concentration of PSS-CQD. The parameters identified are; Excitation: 340 nm, Emission range: 360–550 nm, Excitation slit width: 3.5 nm, Emission slit width: 3.5 nm, scan speed: 100 nm/min. The standards were measured with above parameters. This parameter was used for all the samples collected from laboratory and field testing. The emission intensity data obtained for various standards were correlated with concentrations in 4 different plots (5–100 ppb, 100–1000 ppb, 1–10 ppm and 10–100 ppm) (Supplementary Fig. S1 & Supplementary Table S1a – d) The best fit line drawn for all the plots and the regression equations shows linear correlation between emission intensity and PSS-CQD concentration (Supplementary Fig. S2). The correlation coefficient is very poor for concentration ranging from 5 to 100 ppb. However good correlation is observed for concentrations range 100–1000 ppb, 1–10 ppm and 10–100 ppm with correlation coefficient >0.9. Thus the signal can be clearly picked for concentrations greater than or equal to 100 ppb. The regression equation can be used for determining PSS-CQD concentration of unknown sample. This experiment also enables to use optimum PSS-CQD concentration for laboratory trials and field experiments.

Tritium was detected in high background Liquid scintillation counter; Chloride was estimated by Titrimetric method with silver nitrate solution and potassium dichromate as indicator. CQD fluorophores (CP) was detected by Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer). Data acquisition and instrumental parameters are controlled from FL Winlab software. For CQD -tracer, emission maxima of PSS-CQD suspension were recorded in FL spectrophotometer. Emission maxima for CQDs were recorded at 440 nm while exciting at 340 nm. PSS-CQD water suspensions were prepared at different concentrations. In order to examine the CDQ performance, fluorescence activity was measured for the lab samples as well as for the field samples. The lab samples were collected in the form of leachates released from the bottom of the column at discrete time interval and cumulative for each specific interval of time and similar experimental investigations were carried out for various soil types. The similar characterized PSS-CQDs were used in field experiment with the multi well technique. The same was followed by sample collection. Sample collection was done towards the direction of flow of the tracers at acceptor well. The water samples collected during lab and field experiment were taken to the Fluorescence spectroscopy lab for measuring fluorescence intensity, after thoroughly cleaning the cuvettes, samples were transferred in the order and loaded to the sample holder socket of the spectrometer. Samples were excited at 340 nm with the slit width of 3.5 nm at 100 nm/min of scan speed.

In our previous reports on polystyrene sulphonate-passivated CQDs (PSS-CQD), we have shown that the CQDs can be easily synthesized and can be used without further purifications [35–37]. These PSS-CQDs possess interesting optical and electronic properties. In order to test these CQDs as hydrogeological nano-tracers, we need to evaluate the following specific features of these materials as per literature reports on tracers:
(a)
Scalable synthetic protocol and material characteristics
(b)
Robustness in optical properties
(c)
Detection ability in ground water
(d)
Non-sticking properties; i.e. ability to flow with water

The outcome of the above mentioned tests would be vital towards the applicability of the PSS-CQDs as hydrogeological nano-tracers.
(a)
Scalable synthetic protocol and material characteristics

In order to use PSS-CQDs for hydrogeological applications, it was necessary to prepare them in large scale and maintain similar physical properties. Pyrolysis of the CA-PSS mixture was done at 200 °C, though citric acid starts decomposing from 180 °C onwards. The PSS-CQDs showed strong fluorescence properties when pyrolyzed at 200 °C. The same may be related to the improved annealing of the carbon core. For synthesizing PSS-CQDs in large scale, the challenge was to maintain water dispersibility and emissive property. To our surprise we found that in the muffle furnace, the final product obtained by pyrolysis at 200 °C contained insoluble residue. The same was absent when pyrolysis was done in tubular furnace. We assumed that inside the muffle furnace, the nature of heating is not the same as compared to that inside a tubular furnace. There may be some over oxidation of the PSS as mentioned in literature [38]. The same might also contribute to the overall absorption and emission property of the final CQDs. Thus to minimize precipitate and to make the CQDs highly water dispersible, we changed the molecular weight of PSS to 10 lacks compared to that of 70 K for small scale preparation. The result was improved thermally stable CQDs as shown in TGA data (Fig. 1a). The TGA thermogram shows high temperature degradation for the large scale synthesized samples. For small-scale prepared CQDs, thermal decomposing started around 180 °C. CQDs prepared in large-scale showed decomposing at 282 °C. FTIR spectra of PSS-CQDs prepared separately in muffle furnace and tubular furnace are shown in Fig. 1b. Spectra of both the PSS-CQDs showed CC stretching at 1633 cm− 1. The peak at 1719 cm− 1 in the spectrum for small-scale prepared CQDs represent –COOH group of aliphatic acids. The peak intensity is very low in proportion in CQDs prepared in large-scale. Band at 3452 cm− 1and 1008 cm− 1 represents carboxylic –O-H stretching and bending respectively. Bands at 2927 and 2858 cm− 1 represent sp2 and sp3 CH stretching respectively. The bands correspond to 1125 cm− 1 in both the CQDs represents CO stretching frequency. From the spectra it is clear that there are substantial changes in the PSS-CQDs structure during pyrolysis inside muffle furnace. Almost all carboxylic acid bonds are absent in the large-scale prepared CQDs. We could expect higher carbon content in the pyrolyzed product. Further support of the complete decomposition of –COOH groups of CQDs have been observed from TGA data of the samples (Fig. 1a). Comparing both FTIR and TGA data it could be concluded that un-burnt or incompletely burnt citric acid (representing –COOH groups) may have started decomposing at 180 °C, hence first break point was observed at 180 °C. In our previous reports, each batch of reaction yielded ~ 0.95 g of PSS-CQDs [35–37]. By carefully scaling up the ratio of PSS & citric acid (as mentioned in experimental section), CQDs were prepared in five batches and each batch produced ~ 18 g CQDs.

Fig. 1c shows the excitation–emission (Ex/Em) maxima detected for the large-scale synthesized PSS-CQDs dispersed in Milli-Q water are 350/460 nm. The emission spectra indicate formation of blue emitting CQDs. Emission maxima was quiet sharp and located ~ 450 nm under 360 nm excitation. The emission spectra recorded at various excitation wavelengths indicate wavelength independent emission properties. The excitation spectrum (extreme left) was obtained by fixing the emission at 450 nm. Fig. 1c shows that the excitation and emission spectra are almost mirror images. The blue emission of the CQDs was further shown by illuminating the cuvet containing the water dispersed CQDs shown as pictorial representation in Fig. 1c.

Morphology of the CQDs prepared in large scale was studied with atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 2 shows the 2D AFM image of the CQDs and supplementary Fig. S4 shows the corresponding histogram. The AFM image shows the presence of the bigger ~25 nm particles that represent high molecular weight PSS globules [39]. The 7–10 nm CQDs can be observed in the background whose population is more intense than the polymeric globular structures. The presence of CQDs in the background is further confirmed from the histogram as shown in Fig. S4. The small hump observed in the histogram at ~ 25 nm may be the PSS globules that represent a much smaller proportion of the total population of visible particles in the AFM image.

Purity of the as prepared PSS-CQDs is an important factor as it may limit large scale applications of such materials as tracer. As per literature, fluorescence may either originate from the carbon core or can be formed from a combined effect of the carbon core and the molecular species attached to its surface [38]. The molecular species is a product of partial pyrolysis of the stabilizer PSS during the carbon core formation. Purity of the CQDs was monitored with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Supplementary Fig. S5), NMR spectroscopy (Supplementary Fig. S6) and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1c). NMR spectra of PSS-CQDs shows peaks representing the side chain benzene rings of PSS. The other peaks represent the –CH2– and –SO3H groups that originate from the PSS moiety attached to the surface of the CQDs [35,36]. The emission spectrum shows almost mirror imaged data of the excitation and emission bands (Fig. 1c). A fair amount of Stokes shift is also observed which indicate absence of appreciable impurities in the large-scale produced CQDs [40]. Such levels for purity enable the PSS-CQDs to be used for detection as tracers without further purification.
(b)
Robustness in optical properties

It is well reported that emission properties of any material or compound depend upon its environment and structure [40,41]. CQDs have been reported to have two emission maxima (extrinsic and intrinsic) [42]. The intrinsic band generally occurs at lower energy levels (red-shifted) compared to that of the extrinsic band and is a measure of the surface trap state [42]. As the tracer properties of PSS-CQDs depends on its fluorescence response for detection, it is important to test the stability of intrinsic fluorescence band (intensity and peak position) at different environmental conditions. Fig. 3 shows variation in intrinsic band emission maxima at different pH ranging from 2 to 12. The emission intensity of the intrinsic band is also a measure of the population of surface trap states present in the CQDs. For PSS-CQDs, the surface SO3H groups plays significant role in determining the surface state population. It is thus very obvious that change in pH of the surrounding medium will affect the emission intensity of the intrinsic band. Fig. 3 indicates that maximum emission of PSS-CQDs increase up to pH 5.63 and continuously decreases afterwards. Similar trend of CQDs has been already reported by Karak et al. [43]. The pH dependant variation in emission intensity clearly depicts the right working pH conditions for PSS-CQDs. This information is very important to evaluate the emission intensity of CQDs in porous media. Stable emission is observed in concentrated HCl and NaOH with minor fluctuations even after 15 days (Fig. 4a). The slight blue shift of the emission peak (from 460 nm to 448 nm) observed in acidic pH for CQDs can be related to the surface adsorbed acidic –COOH groups of CQDs. The same has been explained by Karak et al. [43]. The slight decrease in emission intensity along with blue shift at acidic pH may also be related to the change in the surface environment of the CQDs. Similar trend in stability of emission data was observed in strong oxidative and corrosive acids like HNO3 and H2SO4 respectively which indicates robustness in emission properties of the PSS-CQDs. D.Y.Pan et al. have shown FL intensity of surface passivated CQDs containing amine group is nearly constant in the neutral range (5.5–8). The same intensity shows adverse changes in acidic and basic pH [44]. On the other hand, for polyethylene glycol passivated CQDs, emission intensity gradually increases with the shift in pH from acidic to basic region [44]. Liu et al. have reported similar variations in emission intensity with respect to change in pH [45]. The above observations and literature reports indicate that the pH dependency of emission maxima varies with respect to nature of surface passivation [46–48].

Apart from ambient conditions, emission stability was checked at high temperature and pressure. In order to check stability of fluorescence spectra under high temperature and pressure, aqueous dispersion of PSS-CQDs (0.1 mg ml − 1) was sealed in high pressure PARR reactor at 80 °C for 3 h. Fig. 4b shows fluorescence spectra of CQDs before and after heating. Heated samples shows slight decrease in intensity as compare to untreated. Prolonged heating (20h) shows further decrease in intensity that may result from slow reorganization of surface trap states related to Ostwald ripening (Fig. 4b). The changes observed in FL properties before injecting the tracer in ground water and after collection can also help in predicting the environment inside the sub-surface. The change in emission intensity or shift in maxima can also help one to understand whether the change is due to pH, temperature or other factors. All annotations are obviously endorsing that PSS-CQDs to be highly stable in harsh environments and preserves its emission property for longer duration for detection. Hence, the PSS-CQDs can be appropriate option for tracer applications.
(c)
Detection ability in ground water

PSS-CQDs exhibit strong fluorescence property at high dilution as observed in Fig. 5 . In order to study the detection limits of PSS-CQDs, the intrinsic band was recorded with increasing concentrations of suspended PSS-CQDs in water. Fig. 5 show the emission intensity of intrinsic band increases almost linearly up to 0.05 wt% (500 ppm) of PSS-CQDs. There is decrease in intensity observed beyond 500 ppm. The decrease in emission intensity may be due to inner-filter effect as observed in case of fluorescent organic dyes [40]. Beyond 500 ppm, the suspension becomes too dark for fluorescence measurements. Quenching of fluorescent species at certain concentrations is a universal phenomenon displayed by molecular species due to internal collisions and hence deactivation (non-radiative process) [40]. The CQDs show increased absorption in the visible region with increased concentration. The same is termed as inner-filter effect. Thus at high concentration (beyond 500 ppm), the emission band in the visible region is over-shadowed due to that inner filter effect. The measured quantum yield (QY) value of PSS-CQDs was found to be ~ 48%, which can be considered to be descent for its application as fluorescent tracer. QY was measured in neutral aqueous solution using 8-Anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid in methanol. Average QY was obtained from triplicate measurement. The high QY value was due to the possible interactions of the PSS with the carbon surface that may stabilize the surface states of the CQDs resulting in intense intrinsic emission band [42,44].

Fluorescence life time of PSS-CQDs was reported to be around 2.4 ns [37]. The relatively high life time and QY values of PSS-CQDs enable their easy detection in very dilute suspensions as well as through EPI fluorescence microscope. Fig. 6a shows EPI-fluorescence image of drop casted PSS-CQDs under UV-filter. Emissive puncta are more visible under UV-filter and almost negligible under blue and green filters. In case of spin coated and drop-casted samples of PSS-CQDs, emission puncta are visible under all the three filters over dark background (Fig. 6b, c & d). Aquifer samples can be easily drop-casted and studied under simple fluorescence microscope to detect presence of PSS-CQDs as tracers. Such easy detection enables CQDs to be detected during tracer studies.

The above mentioned detection limits of CQDs can be useful to detect it as tracer in presence of humic substances present in aquifers. Literature suggests that natural humic acid shows emission properties in the same region as that of PSS-CQDs [49]. Thus, PSS-CQDs may face issues from the fact that the tracer may not be clearly discernible from the background as it is detected by fluorescence spectroscopy at 340 nm. The emission intensity of CQDs may be influenced by other co-existing compounds like natural humic acid. Literature reports show different excitation–emission (Ex/Em) maxima are detected for humic substances depending on their nature and source in ground water [49]. Ex/Em maxima for marine humic substances are observed at Ex/Em = 260/380–460 nm and 350/420–480 nm. These are peaks for humic substances. Another two protein-like peaks, i.e., tryptophan-like (275/340 nm) and tyrosine-like (275/310 nm) is also observed [49]. Though humic substances possess emission properties similar to CQDs, these natural humic compounds possess weak emissive properties and suffer from low QY values (0.1–2%) and stability issues. Thus, humic acids are difficult to be detected in ppm or ppb levels with normal spectrofluorometer [49,50]. Compared to that, PSS-CQDs with QY value ~48% can be prominently detected in aquifer samples as shown in fluorescence data under 340 nm excitation in ground water sample (depicted in next sections).

Emission from humic substances is quenched by high temperature, pressure and presence of heavy metal ions [51,52]. On the other hand, stability studies have revealed that the emission of PSS-CQDs is not hampered by temperature, pressure and pH (Fig. 3 & 4). In order to have a comparative study, fluorescence experiment was separately performed with PSS-CQDs and humic acid (Fig. 7a, b). Fig. 6 shows that Cu2+ ion was able to fully quench the emission of humic acid, whereas there was some decrease in CQD intensity. The decrease in intensity observed after addition of high amount of Cu2+ was due to electrolyte induced colloidal aggregation of PSS-CQDs [40]. Thus, ground water samples containing high amounts of humic substances can first be treated with Cu2+ or other heavy metals to quench the signal for humic substances and then the same sample can be excited at 340 nm to detect the PSS-CQDs as tracers. The robust emission properties of PSS-CQDs towards changes in pH, temperature, pressure and ionic strength can be advantageous over that of humic substances and can help to detect CQDs in ground water samples.
(d)
Non-sticking properties; i.e. ability to flow with water

In order to evaluate PSS-CQDs as hydrological tracer, it is important to study its sticking properties in presence of various geological samples like soil (different types, sand, rocks etc). Experiments were conducted in laboratory under simulated condition using large diameter and large length PVC columns and in field in granite terrain aquifers. The laboratory experiments were conducted in four columns for four different type of materials such as fine sand, coarse sand, coarse textured red soil and medium textured red soil. Red soils are derived from the weathering of granite formation. In the field, bore hole tests were conducted at a site in granite terrain using CQD tracer to evaluate the performance of the tracer and groundwater flow velocity estimation.

The depiction of solute transport in the vadose zone has been a great center of attraction among the researchers over the few decades. Parameters reveal numerous informations through the transportation in the soils. Since the solute transport in the soil is a complex phenomenon which requires appropriate data to meet the requirements [53]. So, experiments were carried out in fabricated columns packed with different type of soils (Table 1). Injection and extraction of tracers in the actual in-situ condition is a very complex task so, in order to minimize the complexity fabricated columns were prepared to meet the requirement. In the previous studies, dimensions of soil column were mentioned which varies from 10 cm to over 3 m in height and few centimeters to 100 cm in diameter [54]. Keeping in mind, column was prepared with varying texture of soils to test the new CQD (Table 1). The soil column was saturated several times with deionized water for one whole day before the day of the respective experiments. In each column, a sample was collected before the start of the experiment for knowing the background value. Tracers were introduced at the top of the sand column. After 3 h of tracer introduction, deionized water was added from the top continuously with constant head of 10 cm and leachates comes out from the bottom are collected. Water samples were collected at regular interval of time from the time of leachate arrival for tracer concentration. For coarse and fine sands, the leachate arrived after 1 min and 30 min respectively. For coarse and medium textured red soil, the leachate arrived within 5 and 10 min respectively. Fluorescence measurements were done in fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength at 340 nm and emission intensity measured in the range: 360–550 nm. The maximum peak emission intensity was observed at 440 nm. Results of comparative movement of three tracers namely tritium, chloride and CQD in laboratory column (A, B, C & D) experiment is shown in Fig. 8 .

The breakthrough curves (Fig. 8a) obtained by analyzing samples from the laboratory column A shows similar pattern for all the three tracers with clear indication of arrival, peak signal and a steady decrease in concentration (Gaussian type of distribution). Fig. 8a thus shows that the diffusion properties of the CQDs are very similar to the conventional tracers. Few high intensity peaks are noticed for CQD tracer which may be due to late arrivals of few CQD clusters that indicate some stickiness tendency of the CQDs towards the packing material used in column A. Arrival time of tritium and carbon is same (i.e. after 14 h of introduction), while it is only 12.5 h for chloride. Results of comparative movement of two tracers, tritium and CQD in laboratory column B (coarse sand) are shown in (Fig. 8b).

The breakthrough curves (Fig. 8b) in the laboratory column B study indicate similar nature as that observed in column A. The arrival time for both the tracers is much faster in column B than in column A Such observation may be related to the nature of the sand particle present in the column. Slight variation i.e. arrival of peaks at different time interval for CQD might be due to stickiness or diffusion of smaller particle into sand. The stickiness observed in the flow pattern for CQD sample provides more information to hydrogeologists about the nature of soil/sand sample present in the column as well as the porosity of the column. Conventional tracers like Tritium were incapable of extracting data or soil nature information as done by the CQDs.

Results of comparative movement of two tracers, tritium and CQD in laboratory column C (coarse textured red soil) & D (medium textured red soil) experiments are shown in (Fig. 8c&d). In the case of medium textured soil, arrival times of tritium and CQD nano tracers are same (i.e. at 50th min of tracer introduction). Peak activity observed for CQD nano tracer is slightly earlier as compared to tritium. The peak tritium activity observed is at 120th min, while for nano tracer it is 110th min. Thus all the four experiments are successful in terms of behavior of tracer through soil columns. The PSS-CQDs showed very low stickiness in various types of soil and sand samples. This data allows further investigations of the CQDs as tracers in field experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bore hole tracer (aquifer field tracer) tests
ce:section.ce:para:
Natural groundwater flow is usually found to be laminar, which indicates that the velocity of groundwater flow is proportional to the hydraulic gradient. The relationship known as Darcy's law can be expressed as;
Vn = Kj
where,
v = pore velocity of groundwater
n = effective porosity of an aquifer
k = hydraulic conductivity of an aquifer
j = hydraulic gradient

Groundwater velocity can be estimated with the help of single well and multi well methods [12]. Field experiment should be in control for water level fluctuations that generally leads to pronounced clogging [55]. In the single well method, the groundwater column of a filter tube/bore well is labeled with a tracer solution. The decrease in the concentration of this solution in time scale is a function of groundwater flow across the well. The mathematical expression for groundwater filtration velocity (v⁎) is given as
(1)
Q =
V t
In
C 0
C
(2)
V ∗
=
V At
In
C
C 0
(3)
V ∗
= pnv
Where,
Q = groundwater discharge from the filter tube
V = volume of labeled water column
C0 = concentration of the labeled water column at time t = 0
C = concentration of the labeled water column at time t
A = flow cross section area of the tube
v⁎ = filtration velocity
pn = effective porosity of the aquifer
v = distance velocity of groundwater.

In the multi well technique, a labeling substance is added to the groundwater at an injection point (donor well). Detection is carried out in the direction of flow of the tracers at the acceptor wells. The measured parameter is time taken by the labeled water when the flow is from the donor well to the acceptor well. From the flow time (t) and distance (a), the velocity is given by
V =
a t
The borehole tracer method is in principle very simple and has been extensively used in groundwater investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Borehole test for groundwater velocity estimation using CQD tracer
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting tracer studies, the obtained ground water without tracer was subjected to emission studies at different excitations (300–400 nm). Emission spectra (Supplementary Fig. S7) shows very weak background emission dominated with Raman bands. The experiment was done to detect appreciable amounts of humic substances in ground water. The emission data indicated that PSS-CQDs could be easily detected without any background hindrance from humic substances.

Tracer was injected in BW3 for both the experimental investigations within short span of 2 min and thoroughly mixed with the existing well water for 30 min. Submersible pump of 1 HP capacity was installed in BW2 at 40 m depth and run through 2 KVA generator, which act as sampling well for the experiment 1 and existing 5 HP submersible pump fitted in BW 1 at 45 m depth was used for experiment 2 (Table 3 ). Water samples were collected at regular interval of time from the beginning of pumping to the end of pumping for both the experiments. Emission spectra of the samples taken at regular intervals were recorded (Supplementary Fig. S8).

In the first experiment, PSS-CQD tracer injected in a BW3 has shown a peak signal of its arrival in observation well BW2 (at a distance of 18 m from injection well) after 1260 min (Table 4 & Fig. 10a). The velocity of ground water is calculated as 0.854 m/h or 20.5 m/d. In the second experiment nano tracer injected in a BW3 has shown a peak signal of its arrival in observation well BW1 (at a distance of 12 m from injection well) after 785 min (Table 4 & Fig. 10b). The velocity of ground water is calculated as 0.92 m/h or 22.1 m/d. Higher velocity observed in the case of second experiment is due to higher capacity submersible pump fitted in observation well.

BW1 resulting in higher hydraulic gradient. Thus, it can be proved from the above discussion that, the newly developed PSS-CQDs could be effectively used as hydrological tracer for evaluating groundwater flow characteristics.

Finally we conclude that, PSS passivated CQDs were prepared, characterized and tested for future applications as nano-tracres for ground water management. Initially, the PSS-CQDs were separately prepared in small scale and large scale in laboratory. The properties of CQDs obtained from both processes were compared. It was found that the properties of CQDs prepared through scale-up method was very similar to that of the samples prepared in small scale method which shows feasibility of the CQDs to be used as nano-tracers. This was followed by testing of fluorescence properties of PSS-CQDs at various pH, temperatures, pressure and in the presence of corrosive acids and bases. The robustness of the emission property was eminent in the test results. Detailed studies were done to formulate the method of detection of PSS-CQDs in presence of interfering humic substances. Finally, the PSS-CQDs were tested for their stickiness in laboratory simulated soil columns as well as in actual field conditions. The column experiments were done along with conventional tracers to test the comparative arrival curves. The results were quite encouraging and it can be a revolution in groundwater tracer studies. Replacement of conventional tracers with the novel PSS-CQDs can be a significant contribution of nano technology in the field of hydrogeology.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Taufique Warsi:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Lopamudra Bhattacharjee:Data curation, Writing - original draft.S. Thangamani:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Sanjeev K. Jat:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Kallol Mohanta:Writing - review & editing.Rama Ranjan Bhattacharjee:Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.Rangarajan Ramaswamy:Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation.C. Manikyamba:Supervision, Software, Validation.T. Vinoda Rao:Supervision, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Tiwari, Director CSIR-National Geophysical Research Institute, Hyderabad, India, Ministry of Earth Sciences Govt of India and PSG Institute of Advance Studies for their respective supports. The author is thankful to Dr. D. Muralidharan for his kind guidance and also Selles Adrien, George Biswas, Mohd Arshad, Mohd Wajiduddin and Khaja Moinuddin for their unconditional support during lab and field work. RRB thanks HUNTSMAN (India) for their support.

Water Resour. Res.
